topic: Aspects of French-speaking Society: Current Issues
subject: french

items.q: Which verbs take 'être' as their auxiliary in the passé composé?
items.correct: Movement/state-change verbs (DR & MRS VAN DER TRAMP) and all reflexive verbs.
items.why: Students forget reflexives. Both groups use être: aller/venir/monter, etc. AND every reflexive verb (je me suis lavé). With être, the past participle agrees with the subject.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to my friend's house' in French?
items.misconception: Je vais à la maison de mon ami.
items.why: Students translate word-for-word using 'à la maison de'. 'Chez' specifically means 'at the home/place of' someone: chez moi, chez le médecin, chez Pierre. Much more idiomatic and tested.

items.q: What is the difference between 'le' and 'lui' as pronouns?
items.correct: Le replaces a direct object (him/it); lui replaces an indirect object (to him/her).
items.random_wrong: Le is masculine, lui is feminine.
items.why: Students don't distinguish object types. 'Je le vois' (I see him - direct); 'Je lui parle' (I speak TO him - indirect). The verb's preposition determines the pronoun type.

items.q: Which French verb form correctly says 'I would have helped' (j'... aidé)?
items.why: It's aurais. “aurai” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which conjunction always triggers the subjunctive?
items.why: It's bien que. “parce que” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many subjunctive 'triggers' do you typically learn at A-Level (il faut que, je veux que, bien que, pour que, sans que, à condition que)?
items.why: The real figure is 6 triggers. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
